subject: Oral English Language

sub_topics.title: Greetings
competences: Use appropriate greetings at different times of the day

instructional_blocks.hook: Good Morning Greeting
teacher_steps: Introduce the phrase 'Good morning'
Explain the Good morning greeting is used before noon (12 hours)
Demonstrate greeting each other in the morning
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition of 'Good morning'
Pair learners to practice the greeting in a dialogue format
Rotate pairs to ensure everyone practices with different partners
learner_steps: Practice greeting in pairs using dialogue format
Rotate partners and practice with different learners
Role-play morning meeting scenarios in small groups
Perform dialogue in front of the class

instructional_blocks.hook: Good Afternoon Greeting
teacher_steps: Introduce the phrase 'Good afternoon'
Explain the Good afternoon greeting is used after noon (12 hours)
Demonstrate greeting each other in the afternoon
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition of 'Good afternoon'
Pair learners to practice the greeting in a dialogue format
Rotate pairs to ensure everyone practices with different partners
learner_steps: Practice greeting in pairs using dialogue format
Rotate partners and practice with different learners
Role-play afternoon meeting scenarios in small groups
Perform dialogue in front of the class

instructional_blocks.hook: Good Evening Greeting
teacher_steps: Introduce the phrase 'Good evening'
Explain the Good evening greetings is used after afternoon (starting from 18 hours)
Demonstrate greeting each other in the evening
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition of 'Good evening'
Pair learners to practice the greeting in a dialogue format
Rotate pairs to ensure everyone practices with different partners
learner_steps: Practice greeting in pairs using dialogue format
Rotate partners and practice with different learners
Role-play evening meeting scenarios in small groups
Perform dialogue in front of the class

instructional_blocks.hook: How are you? I'm fine/not fine. Good/I'm sorry
teacher_steps: Revise greeting at different times (good morning, afternoon, & evening)
Introduce the phrase 'How are you?' and the various responses (I am fine/ I am not fine)
Explore meaning of 'fine' and 'not fine'. Tell learners to look happy or look sad as they respond
If the response 'fine', we say 'Good'. If the response is 'not fine', we say 'I'm sorry'
Demonstrate common greetings
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition
Pair learners to practice the greeting in a dialogue format
learner_steps: Review greetings at different times of day
Learn phrase 'How are you?' and responses
Practice in pairs with emotion flashcards
Sing 'How are you my partner?' song

instructional_blocks.hook: How is home? Home is fine/not fine. Good/I'm sorry
teacher_steps: Revise greeting at different times (good morning, afternoon, & evening)
Introduce the phrase 'How is home?' and various suitable responses (Home is fine/Home is not fine)
Demonstrate common greetings
Pair learners to practice the greeting in a dialogue format
Rotate pairs to ensure everyone practices with different partners
learner_steps: Learn 'How is home?' phrase and responses
Role-play scenarios based on cards picked

instructional_blocks.hook: Consolidation of Greetings content
teacher_steps: Revise greeting at different times and phrases taught earlier in the week
Demonstrate how to use, and respond to, common greetings
Pair learners to practice the greeting in a dialogue format
Put learners in small groups with flashcards
Ask learners to role-play scenarios
Provide feedback on dialogues performed
learner_steps: Review all greetings learned during the week
Practice full greeting conversations in pairs
Sing 'How are you/How is home my partner?' song

sub_topics.short_notes: This sub-topic focuses on equipping learners with the ability to use appropriate and respond to greetings for different times of the day. Learners will explore and practice greetings commonly used in the morning, afternoon, and evening and how to respond appropriately. Understanding and applying proper greetings is an essential communication skill that fosters respect, builds relationships, and promotes social harmony. The Good morning greeting is used before noon (12 hours). The Good afternoon greeting is used after noon (12 hours). The Good evening greeting is used after afternoon (starting from 18 hours).

vocabulary.definition: A polite word or phrase used when meeting or welcoming someone
The time of day before noon (12 hours)
The time of day after noon (12 hours)
The time of day after afternoon (starting from 18 hours)

questions.standard: Appropriate greetings at different times of the day used correctly

sub_topics.title: Classroom Rules
competences: Use language to talk about classroom rules

instructional_blocks.hook: How do rules help us?
teacher_steps: Discuss rules that the learners have at home
Allow learners to respond and explore other things they are not allowed to do
Explain that rules are designed to help us live in order and peace
Emphasise the need for quiet time, whilst listening to teacher/other learners talking
Demonstrate talking about rules at home and in the classroom
Practice whole class talking about home rules
Put learners in pairs to talk about home rules
learner_steps: Share what they are not allowed to do at home
Practice talking about home rules in pairs
Look at posters depicting rules being broken
Discuss in small groups what rules are being broken
Sing a song on home rules

instructional_blocks.hook: Form classroom rules with learners
teacher_steps: Prepare the classroom with some dirt/rubbish, messy desks arrangement
Ask what is wrong with the classroom
Learners help teacher put things in order
Thank the class for their help
Introduce the phrase 'classroom rules'
Explain the importance of classroom rules
Talk about classroom rules
Practice talking about classroom rules as a class
learner_steps: Help organize and clean the classroom
Practice talking about classroom rules in pairs
Work in small groups to discuss classroom rules
As a class, agree on a set of classroom rules
Write names on classroom rules to show commitment
Sing a song on classroom rules

instructional_blocks.hook: Asking permission – 'May I…?'
teacher_steps: Introduce the phrase 'May I…'
Explain that 'May I…' is the polite way to ask for permission
Demonstrate polite requests
Practice asking for permission politely
Pair learners to practice
Put learners in small groups with flashcards
learner_steps: Practice asking permission politely in pairs
Use emotion and classroom object flashcards
Role-play scenarios based on cards picked
Sing a song on classroom rules

sub_topics.short_notes: This sub-topic focuses on equipping learners with the ability to own and follow classroom rules. Learners will use simple language to talk about classroom rules. Understanding and following classroom rules is an essential communication skill that helps in creating a good learning environment. Rules are designed to help us live in order and peace at home and in the classroom. Classroom rules include: No running in class (We should walk), No fighting (We should be kind to one another), No stealing (We should respect each other's property), No jumping on desks (We should be safe).

vocabulary.definition: Instructions that tell us what we should and should not do
A room where teaching and learning takes place
Asking if you are allowed to do something

questions.format: Roleplay, Demonstrations, Presentations, Question and Answer

sub_topics.title: Family
competences: Use appropriate language to talk about members of a nuclear family

instructional_blocks.hook: Mentioning members of a nuclear family (father, mother, child/children)
teacher_steps: Ask learners who is in their family
Acknowledge that families might look different
Introduce the phrase 'nuclear family'
Explain that a nuclear family has a mother, father and children
Using a poster, identify the mother, father and children
Point to the picture and identify members of the family
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition
Pair learners to practice responding
learner_steps: Share who is in their family
Role-play a family scenario in small groups
Say their role in the family

instructional_blocks.hook: Talk about relationships that exist within the nuclear family (parents, brother, sister)
teacher_steps: Revise the phrase 'nuclear family'
Ask learners to name members of nuclear family
Introduce the relationships (parents, brother, sister)
Explain what we mean by parent/brother/sister
Using a poster, identify the parents, brother and sister
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition
Pair learners to practice responding
learner_steps: Role-play family scenarios with parents, sister, brother

instructional_blocks.hook: Consolidation of nuclear family content
teacher_steps: Recap on members of nuclear family and relationships
Highlight that mother and father are also called parents
Highlight that brother and sister are children of parents
Using a poster, identify all family members and relationships
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition
Pair learners to practice
learner_steps: Review all family members and relationships
Practice full family conversations in pairs
Say each member and their relationship

sub_topics.short_notes: This sub-topic focuses on equipping learners with the ability to use appropriate language to talk about the members of the family. Learners will explore and practice words commonly used when talking about members of the family. Understanding and applying proper use of communication skills that foster respect, builds relationships, and promotes social harmony. A nuclear family is a family that has a mother, father and children. The mother and father are called parents. The children can be brothers and sisters.

vocabulary.definition: A group of people related to each other, especially parents and children
A family that has a mother, father and children
The father and mother
A male child of the same parents
A female child of the same parents

questions.standard: Appropriate language to talk about members of a nuclear family used accordingly

sub_topics.title: Simple commands

instructional_blocks.hook: Responding to simple commands (clap your hands, sit down, jump up and down)
teacher_steps: Introduce the word 'command' and what it means
Demonstrate responding to simple commands
Perform a whole-class repetition
Pair learners to practice responding to simple commands
Rotate pairs to ensure everyone practices with different partners
Put learners into small groups to give and respond to commands
learner_steps: Practice responding to commands as a class
Give and respond to commands in small groups

instructional_blocks.hook: Roleplaying in groups giving and responding to simple commands
teacher_steps: Ask learners about people they know that give commands
Discuss the answers given
Explain that we are going to pretend to be those people and give/respond to commands
Put learners into pairs to role-play
Put learners into small groups to role-play different scenarios
learner_steps: Think about people who give commands
Practice role-playing in pairs (soldier, teacher, etc.)
Respond to command 'jump up and down'

instructional_blocks.hook: Playing games on giving and responding to commands (Simon says)
teacher_steps: Explain the rules of the game 'Simon says'
Demonstrate playing the game
Put learners into pairs to play games
Put learners in small groups to play games
Provide feedback to learners
learner_steps: Play games in small groups (Simon says, robot game, command relay race)
Respond to various commands given by teacher

sub_topics.short_notes: This sub-topic concentrates on equipping learners with the ability to give and follow simple commands. Learners will analyze and practice giving and responding to simple commands. Comprehending and responding to simple commands is an essential communication skill that allows learners to function effectively across different environments.

vocabulary.definition: An instruction to do something

questions.format: Role-play, Demonstrations, Presentations, Question and Answer
questions.standard: Simple commands given and followed accordingly

sub_topics.title: Home Chores and Responsibilities
competences: Use language to discuss chores and responsibilities of family members

instructional_blocks.hook: Name the chores of family members
teacher_steps: Introduce the phrase 'family chores'
Explain the meaning of family chores as routine or daily duty/task/piece of work of a household
Ask learners what chores they do at home
Using a poster with family members doing different chores, ask learners to identify the chores
Explain that 'he' is for males, 'she' is for females and 'they' is for more than one person
Teacher calls learners up to mime a chore
Put learners into small groups to mime chores
learner_steps: Share chores they do at home
Practice saying 'He is sweeping/she is cooking/they are picking papers'
Take turns being teacher asking 'What is he/she/they doing?'

instructional_blocks.hook: Naming the responsibilities of family members
teacher_steps: Introduce the phrase 'family responsibility'
Explain family responsibility as the care and support that family members give one another
Give examples of family responsibilities
Demonstrate a dialogue where learners ask each other questions
Conduct a whole class choral repetition
Learners work in pairs to practice the dialogue
learner_steps: Listen to examples (parents protect/provide/care, children respect/obey)

instructional_blocks.hook: Name and discuss the chores and responsibilities of family members
teacher_steps: Review the phrase 'chores and family responsibility'
Learners recall examples of chores and responsibilities
Display poster depicting different chores and responsibilities
Learners mime the various chores and responsibilities
Learners work in pairs choosing family members to mimic
learner_steps: Review all chores and responsibilities learned
Mime chores and responsibilities in pairs

sub_topics.short_notes: This sub-topic focuses on equipping learners with the ability to use appropriate language to discuss chores and responsibilities of family members. Learners will name and discuss chores and responsibilities of family members. Understanding and taking part in home chores and responsibilities, builds unity, respect and a sense of responsibility to yourself, home and family. Family chores are routine or daily duties/tasks of a household such as cooking, washing dishes, sweeping the house/surrounding, table setting, drying utensils. Family responsibilities are the care and support that family members give one another, such as parents supporting/providing for/caring for the family, children obeying family rules and respecting elderly people.

vocabulary.definition: Routine or daily duties/tasks/pieces of work in a household
The care and support that family members give one another

questions.standard: Appropriate language to discuss chores and responsibilities of family members used accordingly

sub_topics.title: Time
competences: Use appropriate language to tell time of the day, days of the week and months of the year

instructional_blocks.hook: Tell time of the day (morning, afternoon and night)
teacher_steps: Recap on the greetings used at different times of the day
Explain the differences between morning, afternoon and nighttime
Demonstrate telling times of the day using conversation poster
Conduct a whole class repetition of telling the time of the day
Pair learners to practice telling the time of the day
learner_steps: Learn about morning, afternoon and night
Practice telling time of day in pairs
Role-play scenarios indicating specific time of day in small groups
Mention activities done at different times of the day

instructional_blocks.hook: Mention and sequence days of the week
teacher_steps: Recap on the days of the week
Explain the sequencing of the days of the week
Ask learners if there are certain activities they do on set days
Demonstrate mentioning the days of the week using pictorial calendar
Sing the 'Days of the Weeks' song
Pair learners to practice sequencing
learner_steps: Sequence flashcards for each day in small groups
As a class organize days into correct sequence
Sing 'the days of the week song'

instructional_blocks.hook: Mention and sequence months of the year
teacher_steps: Revise the months of the year
Explain the sequencing of the months of the year
Demonstrate mentioning the months using pictorial calendar
Sing 'The months of the year' song
Conduct whole-class choral repetition of sequencing
Pair learners to practice sequencing
learner_steps: Sing 'The months of the year' song
Sequence flashcards for months in small groups
As a class organize months into correct sequence
Sing 'the months of the year song'

sub_topics.short_notes: In this subtopic learners are going to learn how to tell time of the day and to sequence the days of the week and months of the year. Knowledge about this will enable learners to develop time management skills, making it easier for them to follow daily schedules, sequence events and remember responsibilities. This will further enhance language development and help learners feel more in control of their lives.

vocabulary.definition: The measure of when things happen
The early part of the day
The time after noon
The time after afternoon, when it gets dark
A period of 24 hours
A period of seven days
One of the twelve divisions of the year
A period of 365 or 366 days

questions.standard: Appropriate language to tell the times of the day, days of the week and months of the year used correctly

sub_topics.title: Food and meals
competences: Use language to talk about different foods and meals of the day

instructional_blocks.hook: Identifying different types of foods (fruits, vegetables, grains)
teacher_steps: Introduce the different types of food (fruits, vegetables, grains)
Bring different types of food to class
With learner, identify the different types of food
Give out picture flashcards with different foods
In small groups, ask learners to sort and group different foods by their food types
learner_steps: Learn about different types of food
Practice in pairs using flashcards saying 'This is a banana. This is a fruit'
Sort and group foods by type in small groups

instructional_blocks.hook: Mentioning types of meals (breakfast, lunch and supper/dinner)
teacher_steps: Recap on the times of the day and the types of meals
Explain when to eat breakfast, lunch and supper/dinner
Demonstrate mentioning the types of meals
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition
Pair learners to practice mentioning types of meals
learner_steps: Review times of day and types of meals
Learn when to eat each meal
Role-play meal scenarios in small groups

instructional_blocks.hook: Recite a prayer before meals
teacher_steps: Introduce what prayer is
Explain why people pray before meals (Thanking God for the food)
Demonstrate how to pray before meals
Conduct a whole-class choral repetition of prayer
Pair learners to practice reciting the prayer
learner_steps: Understand why we pray before meals
Repeat prayer as a class: 'Thank you Lord for the food, Amen'
Role-play praying before a meal in small groups
Recite the prayer offered before meals

sub_topics.short_notes: In this sub-topic learners are going to learn about different types of food, meals that are eaten at different times of the day and how to recite a prayer before meals. Food is something that is eaten everyday, therefore it is essential that learners have knowledge about the types of food and to identify the type of meal they are eating at that particular time of the day. Different types of food include fruits (banana, orange, mango), vegetables (cabbage, okra, Chinese cabbage), and grains (maize, rice). Types of meals include breakfast (eaten in the morning), lunch (eaten in the afternoon), and supper/dinner (eaten in the evening). Prayer before meals: Thank you Lord for the food. Amen.

vocabulary.definition: Something that people and animals eat to stay alive and healthy
The sweet and fleshy product of a tree or plant that contains seeds
Plants or parts of plants that are eaten as food
Seeds from plants like maize, rice, wheat
The first meal of the day, eaten in the morning
A meal eaten in the middle of the day, in the afternoon
The last meal of the day, eaten in the evening
Words said to God to give thanks or ask for something

questions.standard: Appropriate language to talk about different foods and meals used appropriately

teaching_materials: Conversation Posters illustrating different times of the day
Toys, door, window, books, pencils, desks, shoes
Real food items (oranges, mangoes, okra, cabbage, maize)
Picture flashcards of different food items
Food Chart/Conversation Posters showing different types of food
Plates and examples of real foods
